revision.comment: [[WP:AES|←]]新しいページ: ''''諸国牧'''（しょこくまき）は、[[古代]]の日本において、[[兵部省]]により開発・管轄された[[牧場]]であり、主に[[軍馬]...'
revision.text:
'''諸国牧'''（しょこくまき）は、[[古代]]の日本において、[[兵部省]]により開発・管轄された[[牧場]]であり、主に[[軍馬]]や[[貴族]]への[[貢馬]]、[[駅伝|駅伝馬]]などの供給源とされた。また、諸国牧から集められた馬牛を放し飼いにするため、'''近都牧'''（きんとまき）と呼ばれる牧が九州や畿内周辺に設置された。本稿では、諸国牧と近都牧について記す。 *これらの官牧のほかに[[内厩寮]]が経営した[[勅旨牧]]がある。
== 概要 == 諸国牧・近都牧がいつ設置されたのかは定かではないが、[[飛鳥時代]]から[[奈良時代]]の間だと考えられている。 諸国牧は、[[兵部省]]が所轄するする牧で、[[延喜式]]によると18ヵ国に馬牧24ヵ所、牛牧が12ヵ所、馬牛牧が3ヵ所の計39の牧が設置され、毎年、各牧から馬は5歳に達したものが左右馬寮に送られ、西海道諸国の牧からは大宰府に送られた。この諸国から貢上された馬牛を放し飼いにする為に設けられたのが[[近都牧]]で、主に[[九州]]・[[近畿]]周辺に設置された。
律令制下においては、諸国の牧には、牧の責任者の牧長（ぼくちょう）一名、文書事務にあたる牧帳（まきちょう）一名がおかれ、馬牛100頭ごとに牧子（ぼくし）二名で飼育にあたり、、国司が牧長以下らを指揮・監督していた。中央では、兵馬司が、全国の牧と公私の馬牧を管理し、牧で乗馬に適するものは、軍団に送り裕福な[[兵士]]の家で飼育され、駄馬は、駅伝場として使用されるなど、国家によって騎馬の供給管理を統制するための仕組みがつくられていた。なお、牧長・牧帳・牧子らが牧の経営にあたり、放牧中の馬を痩せさせたり、失ったり、あるいは、母蓄100頭に対して駒犢60頭という規定の増産数に達しない場合は、罰せられ、規定以上に駒犢を増やすことができれば、褒美として稲が与えられた。
==牧と所在地== === 諸国牧 === ;関東地方 *[[下野国]] **[[朱門馬牧]] *[[常陸国]] **[[信太馬牧]] *[[下総国]] **[[高津馬牧]]、[[大結馬牧]]、[[木島馬牧]]、[[長洲馬場]]、[[浮島牛場]] *[[上総国]] **[[大野馬牧]]、[[負野牛牧]] [[新屋牧]] *[[安房国]] **[[白浜馬牧]]、[[粉師馬牧]] *[[相模国]] **[[高野馬牛牧]]
;東海地方 *[[駿河国]] **[[岡野馬牧]]、[[蘇弥馬牧]]
;九州地方 *[[備前国]] **[[長嶋馬牛牧]]
===近都牧=== ;近畿地方 *[[摂津国]] **[[鳥養牧]]、[[豊後国牧]]、[[為奈野牧]] *[[近江国]] **[[甲賀牧]] *[[丹波国]] **[[胡麻牧]] *[[播磨国]] **[[垂水牧]] ;九州地方 *[[大宰府]]
== 納入記録一覧 ==
== 関連項目 == *[[勅旨牧]]
{{Japanese-history-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:しょこくまき}} [[Category:日本の律令制]]

;東海地方 *[[駿河国]] **[[岡野馬牧]]、[[蘇弥馬牧]]
;九州地方 *[[備前国]] **[[長嶋馬牛牧]]
===近都牧=== ;近畿地方 *[[摂津国]] **[[鳥養牧]]、[[豊後国牧]]、[[為奈野牧]] *[[近江国]] **[[甲賀牧]] *[[丹波国]] **[[胡麻牧]] *[[播磨国]] **[[垂水牧]] ;九州地方 *[[大宰府]]

===近都牧=== ;近畿地方 *[[摂津国]] **[[鳥養牧]]、[[豊後国牧]]、[[為奈野牧]] *[[近江国]] **[[甲賀牧]] *[[丹波国]] **[[胡麻牧]] *[[播磨国]] **[[垂水牧 (播磨国)|垂水牧]] ;九州地方 *[[大宰府]]

== 関連項目 == *[[勅旨牧]]
{{Japanese-history-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:しょこくまき}} [[Category:日本の律令制]]

'''諸国牧'''（しょこくまき）は、[[古代]]の日本において、[[兵部省]]により開発・管轄された[[牧場]]であり、主に[[軍馬]]や[[貴族]]への[[貢馬]]、[[駅伝|駅伝馬]]などの供給源とされた。また、諸国牧から集められた馬牛を放し飼いにするため、'''近都牧'''（きんとまき）と呼ばれる牧が九州や畿内周辺に設置された。本稿では、諸国牧と近都牧について記す。 *官牧には、ほかに[[内厩寮]]が経営していた[[勅旨牧]]がある。
== 概要 == 諸国牧・近都牧がいつ設置されたのかは定かではないが、[[飛鳥時代]]から[[奈良時代]]の間だと考えられている。 諸国牧は、[[兵部省]]が所轄するする牧で、[[延喜式]]によると18ヵ国に馬牧が24ヵ所、牛牧が12ヵ所、馬牛牧が3ヵ所の計39の牧が設置され、毎年、各牧から馬は5歳に達したものが左右馬寮に送られ、西海道諸国の牧からは大宰府に送られた。この諸国から貢上された馬牛を放し飼いにする為に設けられたのが[[近都牧]]で、主に[[九州]]・[[近畿]]周辺に設置された。
律令制下においては、諸国の牧には、牧の責任者の[[牧長]]（ぼくちょう）一名、文書事務にあたる[[牧帳]]（まきちょう）一名がおかれ、[[ウマ|馬]][[ウシ|牛]]100頭ごとに[[牧子]]（ぼくし）二名で飼育にあたり、、[[国司]]が牧長以下らを指揮・監督していた。中央では、[[兵馬司]]が、全国の牧と公私の馬牧を管理し、牧で乗馬に適するものは、軍団に送り裕福な[[兵士]]の家で飼育され、駄馬は、駅伝場として使用されるなど、国家によって騎馬の供給管理を統制するための仕組みがつくられていた。なお、牧長・牧帳・牧子らが牧の経営にあたり、放牧中の馬を痩せさせたり、失ったり、あるいは、母蓄100頭に対して駒犢60頭という規定の増産数に達しない場合は、罰せられ、規定以上に駒犢を増やすことができれば、褒美として稲が与えられた。
==牧と所在地== === 諸国牧 === ;関東地方 *[[下野国]] **[[朱門馬牧]] *[[常陸国]] **[[信太馬牧]] *[[下総国]] **[[高津馬牧]]、[[大結馬牧]]、[[木島馬牧]]、[[長洲馬場]]、[[浮島牛場]] *[[上総国]] **[[大野馬牧]]、[[負野牛牧]] [[新屋牧]] *[[安房国]] **[[白浜馬牧]]、[[粉師馬牧]] *[[相模国]] **[[高野馬牛牧]]
;東海地方 *[[駿河国]] **[[岡野馬牧]]、[[蘇弥馬牧]]
;九州地方 *[[備前国]] **[[長嶋馬牛牧]]
===近都牧=== ;近畿地方 *[[摂津国]] **[[鳥養牧]]、[[豊後国牧]]、[[為奈野牧]] *[[近江国]] **[[甲賀牧]] *[[丹波国]] **[[胡麻牧]] *[[播磨国]] **[[垂水牧 (播磨国)|垂水牧]] ;九州地方 *[[大宰府]]
== 納入記録一覧 ==

===近都牧=== *[[摂津国]] **[[鳥養牧]]、[[豊後国牧]]、[[為奈野牧]] *[[近江国]] **[[甲賀牧]] *[[丹波国]] **[[胡麻牧]] *[[播磨国]] **[[垂水牧 (播磨国)|垂水牧]] *[[大宰府]]
== 関連項目 == *[[勅旨牧]]
{{Japanese-history-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:しよこくまき}} [[Category:日本の律令制]] [[Category:日本の牧場|歴しよこくまき]]

{{redirect|牛牧|愛知県名古屋市守山区の地名|牛牧 (名古屋市)}} '''諸国牧'''（しょこくまき）は、[[古代]]の日本において、[[兵部省]]により開発・管轄された[[牧場]]であり、主に[[軍馬]]や[[貴族]]への[[貢馬]]、[[伝馬|駅伝馬]]などの供給源とされた。また、諸国牧から集められた馬牛を放し飼いにするため、'''近都牧'''（きんとまき）と呼ばれる牧が九州や畿内周辺に設置された。本稿では、諸国牧と近都牧について記す。 *官牧には、ほかに[[内厩寮]]が経営していた[[勅旨牧]]がある。
== 概要 == '''諸国牧'''・'''近都牧'''がいつ設置されたのかは定かではないが、[[飛鳥時代]]の頃だと考えられている。 諸国牧は、[[兵部省]]が所轄する牧で、[[延喜式]]によると18ヵ国に馬牧が24ヵ所、牛牧が12ヵ所、馬牛牧が3ヵ所の計39の牧が設置され、毎年、各牧から馬は5歳に達したものが左右馬寮に送られ、西海道諸国の牧からは大宰府に送られた。この諸国から貢上された馬牛を放し飼いにする為に設けられたのが[[近都牧]]で、主に[[九州]]・[[近畿]]周辺に設置された。
律令制下においては、各牧には、牧の責任者の'''[[牧長]]'''（ぼくちょう）1名、文書事務にあたる'''[[牧帳]]'''（まきちょう）1名がおかれ、[[ウマ|馬]][[ウシ|牛]]100頭ごとに'''[[牧子]]'''（ぼくし）二名で飼育にあたり、[[国司]]が牧長以下らを指揮・監督していた。中央では、[[兵馬司]]が、全国の牧と公私の馬牧を管理し、牧で乗馬に適するものは、軍団に送り裕福な[[兵士]]の家で飼育され、駄馬は、駅伝場として使用されるなど、国家によって騎馬の供給管理を統制するための仕組みがつくられていた。なお、牧長・牧帳・牧子らが牧の経営にあたり、放牧中の馬を痩せさせたり、失ったり、あるいは、母蓄100頭に対して駒犢60頭という規定の増産数に達しない場合は、罰せられ、規定以上に駒犢を増やすことができれば、褒美として稲が与えられた。
==牧と所在地== === 諸国牧 === *[[下野国]] **[[朱門馬牧]] *[[常陸国]] **[[信太馬牧]] *[[下総国]] **[[高津馬牧]]、[[大結馬牧]]、[[木島馬牧]]、[[長洲馬場]]、[[浮島牛場]] *[[上総国]] **[[大野馬牧]]、[[負野牛牧]] [[新屋牧]] *[[安房国]] **[[白浜馬牧]]、[[粉師馬牧]] *[[相模国]] **[[高野馬牛牧]] *[[駿河国]] **[[岡野馬牧]]、[[蘇弥馬牧]] *[[備前国]] **[[長嶋馬牛牧]]
===近都牧=== *[[摂津国]] **[[鳥養牧]]、[[豊後国牧]]、[[為奈野牧]] *[[近江国]] **[[甲賀牧]] *[[丹波国]] **[[胡麻牧]] *[[播磨国]] **[[垂水牧 (播磨国)|垂水牧]] *[[大宰府]]
== 関連項目 == *[[勅旨牧]]

revision.comment: /* 近都牧 */ 豊後国牧を豊島牧に書き換え。安田初雄「古代における日本の放牧に関する歴史地理的考察」（福島大学文芸学部論集 第10号 1959年3月）https://ir.lib.fukushima-u.ac.jp/repo/repository/fukuro/R000002116/6-12.pdf の7ページによる。

revision.text:
{{redirect|Ushimaki|For the district in Moriyama Ward, Nagoya, Aichi Prefecture, see {{ill|Ushimaki (Nagoya)|ja|牛牧 (名古屋市)}}}} '''{{ill|Shokoku Maki|ja|諸国牧}}''' (しょこくまき) refers to the pastures developed and managed by the {{ill|Ministry of War (Japan, Ritsuryō system)|ja|兵部省}} in ancient Japan. These pastures primarily served as breeding grounds for {{ill|warhorses|ja|軍馬}}, horses for the aristocracy ({{ill|tribute horses|ja|貢馬}}), and {{ill|post horses|ja|伝馬}} for relay stations. Additionally, to accommodate horses and cattle gathered from the Shokoku Maki, separate pastures called '''{{ill|Kintō Maki|ja|近都牧}}''' were established in Kyushu and around the Kinai region for free-range grazing. This article covers both Shokoku Maki and Kintō Maki.
* Apart from these official pastures, the {{ill|Imperial Stables Bureau|ja|内厩寮}} also managed imperial pastures known as {{ill|Chokushi Maki|ja|勅旨牧}}.
== Overview == The exact time when the '''Shokoku Maki''' and '''Kintō Maki''' were established is unclear, but they are believed to have originated in the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}. Shokoku Maki were pastures under the jurisdiction of the {{ill|Ministry of War (Japan, Ritsuryō system)|ja|兵部省}}. According to the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}, there were 24 horse pastures in 18 provinces, 12 cattle pastures, and 3 combined horse and cattle pastures, totaling 39 official pastures. Each year, five-year-old horses from these pastures were sent to the {{ill|Left and Right Stables Bureau|ja|左右馬寮}}, while those from the pastures in the western provinces ({{ill|Saikaidō|ja|西海道}}) were sent to the {{ill|Dazaifu (Japan)|ja|大宰府}}.
The '''{{ill|Kintō Maki|ja|近都牧}}''' were pastures set up to allow these tribute horses and cattle to graze freely. These were primarily located in {{ill|Kyushu|ja|九州}} and around the {{ill|Kinai|ja|近畿}} region.
Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, each pasture was managed by: - One '''{{ill|Makichō|ja|牧長}}''' (chief overseer of the pasture), - One '''{{ill|Makichō (scribe)|ja|牧帳}}''' (scribe responsible for administrative records), - Two '''{{ill|Makiko|ja|牧子}}''' (caretakers) for every 100 horses or cattle.
The {{ill|Provincial governor|ja|国司}} supervised these officials. At the central government level, the {{ill|Hyōbashi|ja|兵馬司}} oversaw all pastures, both state-owned and private. Horses deemed suitable for riding were sent to military garrisons, where wealthier {{ill|soldiers|ja|兵士}} cared for them, while lower-quality horses were used as relay station mounts. This system ensured state control over the supply and management of cavalry horses.
If pasture officials (Makichō, Makichō scribes, and Makiko caretakers) allowed the horses to become emaciated, lost animals, or failed to meet the mandated increase of 60 foals per 100 breeding mares, they were punished. Conversely, those who exceeded the quota were rewarded with rice.
== Pastures and Locations == ### Shokoku Maki (Provincial Pastures) * **{{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja|下野国}}** - {{ill|Shumon Horse Pasture|ja|朱門馬牧}} * **{{ill|Hitachi Province|ja|常陸国}}** - {{ill|Shida Horse Pasture|ja|信太馬牧}} * **{{ill|Shimōsa Province|ja|下総国}}** - {{ill|Takatsu Horse Pasture|ja|高津馬牧}}, {{ill|Oiyui Horse Pasture|ja|大結馬牧}}, {{ill|Kijima Horse Pasture|ja|木島馬牧}}, {{ill|Nagasu Horse Grounds|ja|長洲馬場}}, {{ill|Ukishima Cattle Grounds|ja|浮島牛場}} * **{{ill|Kazusa Province|ja|上総国}}** - {{ill|Ōno Horse Pasture|ja|大野馬牧}}, {{ill|Onoya Cattle Pasture|ja|負野牛牧}}, {{ill|Araya Pasture|ja|新屋牧}} * **{{ill|Awa Province (Japan)|ja|安房国}}** - {{ill|Shirahama Horse Pasture|ja|白浜馬牧}}, {{ill|Kozu Horse Pasture|ja|粉師馬牧}} * **{{ill|Sagami Province|ja|相模国}}** - {{ill|Takano Horse and Cattle Pasture|ja|高野馬牛牧}} * **{{ill|Suruga Province|ja|駿河国}}** - {{ill|Okano Horse Pasture|ja|岡野馬牧}}, {{ill|Somi Horse Pasture|ja|蘇弥馬牧}} * **{{ill|Bizen Province|ja|備前国}}** - {{ill|Nagashima Horse and Cattle Pasture|ja|長嶋馬牛牧}}
### Kintō Maki (Capital-Adjacent Pastures) * **{{ill|Settsu Province|ja|摂津国}}** - {{ill|Torigai Pasture|ja|鳥養牧}}, {{ill|Toyoshima Pasture|ja|豊島牧}}, {{ill|Inano Pasture|ja|為奈野牧}} * **{{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国}}** - {{ill|Kōga Pasture|ja|甲賀牧}} * **{{ill|Tanba Province|ja|丹波国}}** - {{ill|Goma Pasture|ja|胡麻牧}} * **{{ill|Harima Province|ja|播磨国}}** - {{ill|Tarumi Pasture|ja|垂水牧 (播磨国)}} * **{{ill|Dazaifu (Japan)|ja|大宰府}}**
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Chokushi Maki|ja|勅旨牧}}

According to the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}, there were 24 horse pastures in 18 provinces, 12 cattle pastures, and 3 combined horse and cattle pastures, totaling 39 official pastures. Each year, five-year-old horses from these pastures were sent to the {{ill|Left and Right Stables Bureau|ja|左右馬寮}}, while those from the pastures in the western provinces ({{ill|Saikaidō|ja|西海道}}) were sent to the {{ill|Dazaifu (Japan)|ja|大宰府}}.
The '''{{ill|Kintō Maki|ja|近都牧}}''' were pastures set up to allow these tribute horses and cattle to graze freely. These were primarily located in {{ill|Kyushu|ja|九州}} and around the {{ill|Kinai|ja|近畿}} region.
Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, each pasture was managed by: - One '''{{ill|Makichō|ja|牧長}}''' (chief overseer of the pasture), - One '''{{ill|Makichō (scribe)|ja|牧帳}}''' (scribe responsible for administrative records), - Two '''{{ill|Makiko|ja|牧子}}''' (caretakers) for every 100 horses or cattle.
The {{ill|Provincial governor|ja|国司}} supervised these officials. At the central government level, the {{ill|Hyōbashi|ja|兵馬司}} oversaw all pastures, both state-owned and private. Horses deemed suitable for riding were sent to military garrisons, where wealthier {{ill|soldiers|ja|兵士}} cared for them, while lower-quality horses were used as relay station mounts. This system ensured state control over the supply and management of cavalry horses.
If pasture officials (Makichō, Makichō scribes, and Makiko caretakers) allowed the horses to become emaciated, lost animals, or failed to meet the mandated increase of 60 foals per 100 breeding mares, they were punished. Conversely, those who exceeded the quota were rewarded with rice.

== Pastures and Locations == ### Shokoku Maki (Provincial Pastures) * **{{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja|下野国}}** - {{ill|Shumon Horse Pasture|ja|朱門馬牧}} * **{{ill|Hitachi Province|ja|常陸国}}** - {{ill|Shida Horse Pasture|ja|信太馬牧}} * **{{ill|Shimōsa Province|ja|下総国}}** - {{ill|Takatsu Horse Pasture|ja|高津馬牧}}, {{ill|Oiyui Horse Pasture|ja|大結馬牧}}, {{ill|Kijima Horse Pasture|ja|木島馬牧}}, {{ill|Nagasu Horse Grounds|ja|長洲馬場}}, {{ill|Ukishima Cattle Grounds|ja|浮島牛場}} * **{{ill|Kazusa Province|ja|上総国}}** - {{ill|Ōno Horse Pasture|ja|大野馬牧}}, {{ill|Onoya Cattle Pasture|ja|負野牛牧}}, {{ill|Araya Pasture|ja|新屋牧}} * **{{ill|Awa Province (Japan)|ja|安房国}}** - {{ill|Shirahama Horse Pasture|ja|白浜馬牧}}, {{ill|Kozu Horse Pasture|ja|粉師馬牧}} * **{{ill|Sagami Province|ja|相模国}}** - {{ill|Takano Horse and Cattle Pasture|ja|高野馬牛牧}} * **{{ill|Suruga Province|ja|駿河国}}** - {{ill|Okano Horse Pasture|ja|岡野馬牧}}, {{ill|Somi Horse Pasture|ja|蘇弥馬牧}} * **{{ill|Bizen Province|ja|備前国}}** - {{ill|Nagashima Horse and Cattle Pasture|ja|長嶋馬牛牧}}

[[ja:%E8%AB%B8%E5%9B%BD%E7%89%A7]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Shokoku Maki-qq]] [[Category:Shokoku Maki]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Shokoku Maki-qq]] [[Category:Shokoku Maki]][[Category:Shokoku Maki]] [[Category:Shokoku Maki]]

{{redirect|Ushimaki|For the district in Moriyama Ward, Nagoya, Aichi Prefecture, see {{ill|Ushimaki (Nagoya)|ja|牛牧 (名古屋市)}}}} '''{{ill|Shokoku Maki|ja|諸国牧}}''' (しょこくまき) refers to the pastures developed and managed by the {{ill|Ministry of War (Japan, Ritsuryō system)|ja|兵部省}} in ancient Japan. These pastures primarily served as breeding grounds for {{ill|warhorses|ja|軍馬}}, horses for the aristocracy ({{ill|tribute horses|ja|貢馬}}), and {{ill|post horses|ja|伝馬}} for relay stations. Additionally, to accommodate horses and cattle gathered from the Shokoku Maki, separate pastures called '''{{ill|Kintō Maki|ja|近都牧}}''' were established in Kyushu and around the Kinai region for free-range grazing. This article covers both Shokoku Maki and Kintō Maki.
* Apart from these official pastures, the {{ill|Imperial Stables Bureau|ja|内厩寮}} also managed imperial pastures known as {{ill|Chokushi Maki|ja|勅旨牧}}.
== Overview == The exact time when the '''Shokoku Maki''' and '''Kintō Maki''' were established is unclear, but they are believed to have originated in the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}. Shokoku Maki were pastures under the jurisdiction of the {{ill|Ministry of War (Japan, Ritsuryō system)|ja|兵部省}}. According to the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}, there were 24 horse pastures in 18 provinces, 12 cattle pastures, and 3 combined horse and cattle pastures, totaling 39 official pastures. Each year, five-year-old horses from these pastures were sent to the {{ill|Left and Right Stables Bureau|ja|左右馬寮}}, while those from the pastures in the western provinces ({{ill|Saikaidō|ja|西海道}}) were sent to the {{ill|Dazaifu (Japan)|ja|大宰府}}.
The '''{{ill|Kintō Maki|ja|近都牧}}''' were pastures set up to allow these tribute horses and cattle to graze freely. These were primarily located in {{ill|Kyushu|ja|九州}} and around the {{ill|Kinai|ja|近畿}} region.
Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, each pasture was managed by: - One '''{{ill|Makichō|ja|牧長}}''' (chief overseer of the pasture), - One '''{{ill|Makichō (scribe)|ja|牧帳}}''' (scribe responsible for administrative records), - Two '''{{ill|Makiko|ja|牧子}}''' (caretakers) for every 100 horses or cattle.
The {{ill|Provincial governor|ja|国司}} supervised these officials. At the central government level, the {{ill|Hyōbashi|ja|兵馬司}} oversaw all pastures, both state-owned and private. Horses deemed suitable for riding were sent to military garrisons, where wealthier {{ill|soldiers|ja|兵士}} cared for them, while lower-quality horses were used as relay station mounts. This system ensured state control over the supply and management of cavalry horses.
If pasture officials (Makichō, Makichō scribes, and Makiko caretakers) allowed the horses to become emaciated, lost animals, or failed to meet the mandated increase of 60 foals per 100 breeding mares, they were punished. Conversely, those who exceeded the quota were rewarded with rice.
== Pastures and Locations == ### Shokoku Maki (Provincial Pastures) * **{{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja|下野国}}** - {{ill|Shumon Horse Pasture|ja|朱門馬牧}} * **{{ill|Hitachi Province|ja|常陸国}}** - {{ill|Shida Horse Pasture|ja|信太馬牧}} * **{{ill|Shimōsa Province|ja|下総国}}** - {{ill|Takatsu Horse Pasture|ja|高津馬牧}}, {{ill|Oiyui Horse Pasture|ja|大結馬牧}}, {{ill|Kijima Horse Pasture|ja|木島馬牧}}, {{ill|Nagasu Horse Grounds|ja|長洲馬場}}, {{ill|Ukishima Cattle Grounds|ja|浮島牛場}} * **{{ill|Kazusa Province|ja|上総国}}** - {{ill|Ōno Horse Pasture|ja|大野馬牧}}, {{ill|Onoya Cattle Pasture|ja|負野牛牧}}, {{ill|Araya Pasture|ja|新屋牧}} * **{{ill|Awa Province (Japan)|ja|安房国}}** - {{ill|Shirahama Horse Pasture|ja|白浜馬牧}}, {{ill|Kozu Horse Pasture|ja|粉師馬牧}} * **{{ill|Sagami Province|ja|相模国}}** - {{ill|Takano Horse and Cattle Pasture|ja|高野馬牛牧}} * **{{ill|Suruga Province|ja|駿河国}}** - {{ill|Okano Horse Pasture|ja|岡野馬牧}}, {{ill|Somi Horse Pasture|ja|蘇弥馬牧}} * **{{ill|Bizen Province|ja|備前国}}** - {{ill|Nagashima Horse and Cattle Pasture|ja|長嶋馬牛牧}}
### Kintō Maki (Capital-Adjacent Pastures) * **{{ill|Settsu Province|ja|摂津国}}** - {{ill|Torigai Pasture|ja|鳥養牧}}, {{ill|Toyoshima Pasture|ja|豊島牧}}, {{ill|Inano Pasture|ja|為奈野牧}} * **{{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国}}** - {{ill|Kōga Pasture|ja|甲賀牧}} * **{{ill|Tanba Province|ja|丹波国}}** - {{ill|Goma Pasture|ja|胡麻牧}} * **{{ill|Harima Province|ja|播磨国}}** - {{ill|Tarumi Pasture|ja|垂水牧 (播磨国)}} * **{{ill|Dazaifu (Japan)|ja|大宰府}}**

[[ja:%E8%AB%B8%E5%9B%BD%E7%89%A7]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Shokoku Maki-qq]] [[Category:Shokoku Maki]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Shokoku Maki-qq]] [[Category:Shokoku Maki]][[Category:Shokoku Maki]] [[Category:Shokoku Maki]] [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]]

{{redirect|Ushimaki|For the district in Moriyama Ward, Nagoya, Aichi Prefecture, see {{ill|Ushimaki (Nagoya)|ja|牛牧 (名古屋市)}}}} '''{{ill|Shokoku Maki|ja|諸国牧}}''' (しょこくまき) refers to the pastures developed and managed by the {{ill|Ministry of War (Japan, Ritsuryō system)|ja|兵部省}} in ancient Japan. These pastures primarily served as breeding grounds for {{ill|warhorses|ja|軍馬}}, horses for the aristocracy ({{ill|tribute horses|ja|貢馬}}), and {{ill|post horses|ja|伝馬}} for relay stations. Additionally, to accommodate horses and cattle gathered from the Shokoku Maki, separate pastures called '''{{ill|Kintō Maki|ja|近都牧}}''' were established in Kyushu and around the Kinai region for free-range grazing. This article covers both Shokoku Maki and Kintō Maki.
* Apart from these official pastures, the {{ill|Imperial Stables Bureau|ja|内厩寮}} also managed imperial pastures known as {{ill|Chokushi Maki|ja|勅旨牧}}.
== Overview == The exact time when the '''Shokoku Maki''' and '''Kintō Maki''' were established is unclear, but they are believed to have originated in the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}. Shokoku Maki were pastures under the jurisdiction of the {{ill|Ministry of War (Japan, Ritsuryō system)|ja|兵部省}}. According to the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}, there were 24 horse pastures in 18 provinces, 12 cattle pastures, and 3 combined horse and cattle pastures, totaling 39 official pastures. Each year, five-year-old horses from these pastures were sent to the {{ill|Left and Right Stables Bureau|ja|左右馬寮}}, while those from the pastures in the western provinces ({{ill|Saikaidō|ja|西海道}}) were sent to the {{ill|Dazaifu (Japan)|ja|大宰府}}.
The '''{{ill|Kintō Maki|ja|近都牧}}''' were pastures set up to allow these tribute horses and cattle to graze freely. These were primarily located in {{ill|Kyushu|ja|九州}} and around the {{ill|Kinai|ja|近畿}} region.
Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, each pasture was managed by: - One '''{{ill|Makichō|ja|牧長}}''' (chief overseer of the pasture), - One '''{{ill|Makichō (scribe)|ja|牧帳}}''' (scribe responsible for administrative records), - Two '''{{ill|Makiko|ja|牧子}}''' (caretakers) for every 100 horses or cattle.
The {{ill|Provincial governor|ja|国司}} supervised these officials. At the central government level, the {{ill|Hyōbashi|ja|兵馬司}} oversaw all pastures, both state-owned and private. Horses deemed suitable for riding were sent to military garrisons, where wealthier {{ill|soldiers|ja|兵士}} cared for them, while lower-quality horses were used as relay station mounts. This system ensured state control over the supply and management of cavalry horses.
If pasture officials (Makichō, Makichō scribes, and Makiko caretakers) allowed the horses to become emaciated, lost animals, or failed to meet the mandated increase of 60 foals per 100 breeding mares, they were punished. Conversely, those who exceeded the quota were rewarded with rice.

### Kintō Maki (Capital-Adjacent Pastures) * **{{ill|Settsu Province|ja|摂津国}}** - {{ill|Torigai Pasture|ja|鳥養牧}}, {{ill|Toyoshima Pasture|ja|豊島牧}}, {{ill|Inano Pasture|ja|為奈野牧}} * **{{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国}}** - {{ill|Kōga Pasture|ja|甲賀牧}} * **{{ill|Tanba Province|ja|丹波国}}** - {{ill|Goma Pasture|ja|胡麻牧}} * **{{ill|Harima Province|ja|播磨国}}** - {{ill|Tarumi Pasture|ja|垂水牧 (播磨国)}} * **{{ill|Dazaifu (Japan)|ja|大宰府}}**

== Pastures and Locations == ### Shokoku Maki (Provincial Pastures) * **{{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja|下野国}}** - {{ill|Shumon Horse Pasture|ja|朱門馬牧}} * **{{ill|Hitachi Province|ja|常陸国}}** - {{ill|Shida Horse Pasture|ja|信太馬牧}} * **{{ill|Shimōsa Province|ja|下総国}}** - {{ill|Takatsu Horse Pasture|ja|高津馬牧}}, {{ill|Oiyui Horse Pasture|ja|大結馬牧}}, {{ill|Kijima Horse Pasture|ja|木島馬牧}}, {{ill|Nagasu Horse Grounds|ja|長洲馬場}}, {{ill|Ukishima Cattle Grounds|ja|浮島牛場}} * **{{ill|Kazusa Province|ja|上総国}}** - {{ill|Ōno Horse Pasture|ja|大野馬牧}}, {{ill|Onoya Cattle Pasture|ja|負野牛牧}}, {{ill|Araya Pasture|ja|新屋牧}} * **{{ill|Awa Province (Japan)|ja|安房国}}** - {{ill|Shirahama Horse Pasture|ja|白浜馬牧}}, {{ill|Kozu Horse Pasture|ja|粉師馬牧}} * **{{ill|Sagami Province|ja|相模国}}** - {{ill|Takano Horse and Cattle Pasture|ja|高野馬牛牧}} * **{{ill|Suruga Province|ja|駿河国}}** - {{ill|Okano Horse Pasture|ja|岡野馬牧}}, {{ill|Somi Horse Pasture|ja|蘇弥馬牧}} * **{{ill|Bizen Province|ja|備前国}}** - {{ill|Nagashima Horse and Cattle Pasture|ja|長嶋馬牛牧}}
### Kintō Maki (Capital-Adjacent Pastures) * **{{ill|Settsu Province|ja|摂津国}}** - {{ill|Torigai Pasture|ja|鳥養牧}}, {{ill|Toyoshima Pasture|ja|豊島牧}}, {{ill|Inano Pasture|ja|為奈野牧}} * **{{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国}}** - {{ill|Kōga Pasture|ja|甲賀牧}} * **{{ill|Tanba Province|ja|丹波国}}** - {{ill|Goma Pasture|ja|胡麻牧}} * **{{ill|Harima Province|ja|播磨国}}** - {{ill|Tarumi Pasture|ja|垂水牧 (播磨国)}} * **{{ill|Dazaifu (Japan)|ja|大宰府}}**

{{redirect|Ushimaki|For the district in Moriyama Ward, Nagoya, Aichi Prefecture, see {{ill|Ushimaki (Nagoya)|ja|牛牧 (名古屋市)}}}} '''{{ill|Shokoku Maki|ja|諸国牧}}''' (しょこくまき) refers to the pastures developed and managed by the {{ill|Ministry of War (Japan, Ritsuryō system)|ja|兵部省}} in ancient Japan. These pastures primarily served as breeding grounds for {{ill|warhorses|ja|軍馬}}, horses for the aristocracy ({{ill|tribute horses|ja|貢馬}}), and {{ill|post horses|ja|伝馬}} for relay stations. Additionally, to accommodate horses and cattle gathered from the Shokoku Maki, separate pastures called '''{{ill|Kintō Maki|ja|近都牧}}''' were established in Kyushu and around the Kinai region for free-range grazing. This article covers both Shokoku Maki and Kintō Maki.
* Apart from these official pastures, the {{ill|Imperial Stables Bureau|ja|内厩寮}} also managed imperial pastures known as {{ill|Chokushi Maki|ja|勅旨牧}}.
== Overview == The exact time when the '''Shokoku Maki''' and '''Kintō Maki''' were established is unclear, but they are believed to have originated in the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}. Shokoku Maki were pastures under the jurisdiction of the {{ill|Ministry of War (Japan, Ritsuryō system)|ja|兵部省}}. According to the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}, there were 24 horse pastures in 18 provinces, 12 cattle pastures, and 3 combined horse and cattle pastures, totaling 39 official pastures. Each year, five-year-old horses from these pastures were sent to the {{ill|Left and Right Stables Bureau|ja|左右馬寮}}, while those from the pastures in the western provinces ({{ill|Saikaidō|ja|西海道}}) were sent to the {{ill|Dazaifu (Japan)|ja|大宰府}}.
The '''{{ill|Kintō Maki|ja|近都牧}}''' were pastures set up to allow these tribute horses and cattle to graze freely. These were primarily located in {{ill|Kyushu|ja|九州}} and around the {{ill|Kinai|ja|近畿}} region.
Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, each pasture was managed by: - One '''{{ill|Makichō|ja|牧長}}''' (chief overseer of the pasture), - One '''{{ill|Makichō (scribe)|ja|牧帳}}''' (scribe responsible for administrative records), - Two '''{{ill|Makiko|ja|牧子}}''' (caretakers) for every 100 horses or cattle.
The {{ill|Provincial governor|ja|国司}} supervised these officials. At the central government level, the {{ill|Hyōbashi|ja|兵馬司}} oversaw all pastures, both state-owned and private. Horses deemed suitable for riding were sent to military garrisons, where wealthier {{ill|soldiers|ja|兵士}} cared for them, while lower-quality horses were used as relay station mounts. This system ensured state control over the supply and management of cavalry horses.
If pasture officials (Makichō, Makichō scribes, and Makiko caretakers) allowed the horses to become emaciated, lost animals, or failed to meet the mandated increase of 60 foals per 100 breeding mares, they were punished. Conversely, those who exceeded the quota were rewarded with rice.
== Pastures and Locations == ### Shokoku Maki (Provincial Pastures) * **{{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja|下野国}}** - {{ill|Shumon Horse Pasture|ja|朱門馬牧}} * **{{ill|Hitachi Province|ja|常陸国}}** - {{ill|Shida Horse Pasture|ja|信太馬牧}} * **{{ill|Shimōsa Province|ja|下総国}}** - {{ill|Takatsu Horse Pasture|ja|高津馬牧}}, {{ill|Oiyui Horse Pasture|ja|大結馬牧}}, {{ill|Kijima Horse Pasture|ja|木島馬牧}}, {{ill|Nagasu Horse Grounds|ja|長洲馬場}}, {{ill|Ukishima Cattle Grounds|ja|浮島牛場}} * **{{ill|Kazusa Province|ja|上総国}}** - {{ill|Ōno Horse Pasture|ja|大野馬牧}}, {{ill|Onoya Cattle Pasture|ja|負野牛牧}}, {{ill|Araya Pasture|ja|新屋牧}} * **{{ill|Awa Province (Japan)|ja|安房国}}** - {{ill|Shirahama Horse Pasture|ja|白浜馬牧}}, {{ill|Kozu Horse Pasture|ja|粉師馬牧}} * **{{ill|Sagami Province|ja|相模国}}** - {{ill|Takano Horse and Cattle Pasture|ja|高野馬牛牧}} * **{{ill|Suruga Province|ja|駿河国}}** - {{ill|Okano Horse Pasture|ja|岡野馬牧}}, {{ill|Somi Horse Pasture|ja|蘇弥馬牧}} * **{{ill|Bizen Province|ja|備前国}}** - {{ill|Nagashima Horse and Cattle Pasture|ja|長嶋馬牛牧}}
### Kintō Maki (Capital-Adjacent Pastures) * **{{ill|Settsu Province|ja|摂津国}}** - {{ill|Torigai Pasture|ja|鳥養牧}}, {{ill|Toyoshima Pasture|ja|豊島牧}}, {{ill|Inano Pasture|ja|為奈野牧}} * **{{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国}}** - {{ill|Kōga Pasture|ja|甲賀牧}} * **{{ill|Tanba Province|ja|丹波国}}** - {{ill|Goma Pasture|ja|胡麻牧}} * **{{ill|Harima Province|ja|播磨国}}** - {{ill|Tarumi Pasture|ja|垂水牧 (播磨国)}} * **{{ill|Dazaifu (Japan)|ja|大宰府}}**
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Chokushi Maki|ja|勅旨牧}}

Shokoku Maki were pastures under the jurisdiction of the {{ill|Ministry of War (Japan, Ritsuryō system)|ja|兵部省}}. According to the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}, there were 24 horse pastures in 18 provinces, 12 cattle pastures, and 3 combined horse and cattle pastures, totaling 39 official pastures. Each year, five-year-old horses from these pastures were sent to the {{ill|Left and Right Stables Bureau|ja|左右馬寮}}, while those from the pastures in the western provinces ({{ill|Saikaidō|ja|西海道}}) were sent to the {{ill|Dazaifu (Japan)|ja|大宰府}}.
The '''{{ill|Kintō Maki|ja|近都牧}}''' were pastures set up to allow these tribute horses and cattle to graze freely. These were primarily located in {{ill|Kyushu|ja|九州}} and around the {{ill|Kinai|ja|近畿}} region.
Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, each pasture was managed by: - One '''{{ill|Makichō|ja|牧長}}''' (chief overseer of the pasture), - One '''{{ill|Makichō (scribe)|ja|牧帳}}''' (scribe responsible for administrative records), - Two '''{{ill|Makiko|ja|牧子}}''' (caretakers) for every 100 horses or cattle.
The {{ill|Provincial governor|ja|国司}} supervised these officials. At the central government level, the {{ill|Hyōbashi|ja|兵馬司}} oversaw all pastures, both state-owned and private. Horses deemed suitable for riding were sent to military garrisons, where wealthier {{ill|soldiers|ja|兵士}} cared for them, while lower-quality horses were used as relay station mounts. This system ensured state control over the supply and management of cavalry horses.
If pasture officials (Makichō, Makichō scribes, and Makiko caretakers) allowed the horses to become emaciated, lost animals, or failed to meet the mandated increase of 60 foals per 100 breeding mares, they were punished. Conversely, those who exceeded the quota were rewarded with rice.

{{redirect|Ushimaki|For the district in Moriyama Ward, Nagoya, Aichi Prefecture, see {{ill|Ushimaki (Nagoya)|ja|牛牧 (名古屋市)}}}}
'''{{ill|Shokoku Maki|ja|諸国牧}}''' (しょこくまき) refers to the pastures developed and managed by the {{ill|Ministry of War (Japan, Ritsuryō system)|ja|兵部省}} in ancient Japan. These pastures primarily served as breeding grounds for {{ill|warhorses|ja|軍馬}}, horses for the aristocracy ({{ill|tribute horses|ja|貢馬}}), and {{ill|post horses|ja|伝馬}} for relay stations. Additionally, to accommodate horses and cattle gathered from the Shokoku Maki, separate pastures called '''{{ill|Kintō Maki|ja|近都牧}}''' were established in Kyushu and around the Kinai region for free-range grazing. This article covers both Shokoku Maki and Kintō Maki.
* Apart from these official pastures, the {{ill|Imperial Stables Bureau|ja|内厩寮}} also managed imperial pastures known as {{ill|Chokushi Maki|ja|勅旨牧}}.
== Overview ==
The exact time when the '''Shokoku Maki''' and '''Kintō Maki''' were established is unclear, but they are believed to have originated in the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}.
Shokoku Maki were pastures under the jurisdiction of the {{ill|Ministry of War (Japan, Ritsuryō system)|ja|兵部省}}. According to the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}, there were 24 horse pastures in 18 provinces, 12 cattle pastures, and 3 combined horse and cattle pastures, totaling 39 official pastures. Each year, five-year-old horses from these pastures were sent to the {{ill|Left and Right Stables Bureau|ja|左右馬寮}}, while those from the pastures in the western provinces ({{ill|Saikaidō|ja|西海道}}) were sent to the {{ill|Dazaifu (Japan)|ja|大宰府}}.
The '''{{ill|Kintō Maki|ja|近都牧}}''' were pastures set up to allow these tribute horses and cattle to graze freely. These were primarily located in {{ill|Kyushu|ja|九州}} and around the {{ill|Kinai|ja|近畿}} region.
Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, each pasture was managed by:
- One '''{{ill|Makichō|ja|牧長}}''' (chief overseer of the pasture),
- One '''{{ill|Makichō (scribe)|ja|牧帳}}''' (scribe responsible for administrative records),
- Two '''{{ill|Makiko|ja|牧子}}''' (caretakers) for every 100 horses or cattle.
The {{ill|Provincial governor|ja|国司}} supervised these officials.
At the central government level, the {{ill|Hyōbashi|ja|兵馬司}} oversaw all pastures, both state-owned and private. Horses deemed suitable for riding were sent to military garrisons, where wealthier {{ill|soldiers|ja|兵士}} cared for them, while lower-quality horses were used as relay station mounts. This system ensured state control over the supply and management of cavalry horses.
If pasture officials (Makichō, Makichō scribes, and Makiko caretakers) allowed the horses to become emaciated, lost animals, or failed to meet the mandated increase of 60 foals per 100 breeding mares, they were punished. Conversely, those who exceeded the quota were rewarded with rice.
== Pastures and Locations ==
=== Shokoku Maki (Provincial Pastures)
* '''{{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja|下野国}}'''
- {{ill|Shumon Horse Pasture|ja|朱門馬牧}}
* '''{{ill|Hitachi Province|ja|常陸国}}'''
- {{ill|Shida Horse Pasture|ja|信太馬牧}}
* '''{{ill|Shimōsa Province|ja|下総国}}'''
- {{ill|Takatsu Horse Pasture|ja|高津馬牧}}, {{ill|Oiyui Horse Pasture|ja|大結馬牧}}, {{ill|Kijima Horse Pasture|ja|木島馬牧}}, {{ill|Nagasu Horse Grounds|ja|長洲馬場}}, {{ill|Ukishima Cattle Grounds|ja|浮島牛場}}
* '''{{ill|Kazusa Province|ja|上総国}}'''
- {{ill|Ōno Horse Pasture|ja|大野馬牧}}, {{ill|Onoya Cattle Pasture|ja|負野牛牧}}, {{ill|Araya Pasture|ja|新屋牧}}
* '''{{ill|Awa Province (Japan)|ja|安房国}}'''
- {{ill|Shirahama Horse Pasture|ja|白浜馬牧}}, {{ill|Kozu Horse Pasture|ja|粉師馬牧}}
* '''{{ill|Sagami Province|ja|相模国}}'''
- {{ill|Takano Horse and Cattle Pasture|ja|高野馬牛牧}}
* '''{{ill|Suruga Province|ja|駿河国}}'''
- {{ill|Okano Horse Pasture|ja|岡野馬牧}}, {{ill|Somi Horse Pasture|ja|蘇弥馬牧}}
* '''{{ill|Bizen Province|ja|備前国}}'''
- {{ill|Nagashima Horse and Cattle Pasture|ja|長嶋馬牛牧}}
=== Kintō Maki (Capital-Adjacent Pastures)
* '''{{ill|Settsu Province|ja|摂津国}}'''
- {{ill|Torigai Pasture|ja|鳥養牧}}, {{ill|Toyoshima Pasture|ja|豊島牧}}, {{ill|Inano Pasture|ja|為奈野牧}}
* '''{{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国}}'''
- {{ill|Kōga Pasture|ja|甲賀牧}}
* '''{{ill|Tanba Province|ja|丹波国}}'''
- {{ill|Goma Pasture|ja|胡麻牧}}
* '''{{ill|Harima Province|ja|播磨国}}'''
- {{ill|Tarumi Pasture|ja|垂水牧 (播磨国)}}
* '''{{ill|Dazaifu (Japan)|ja|大宰府}}'''
== See Also ==
* {{ill|Chokushi Maki|ja|勅旨牧}}

=== === Shokoku Maki (Provincial Pastures) === * '''{{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja|下野国}}''' - {{ill|Shumon Horse Pasture|ja|朱門馬牧}} * '''{{ill|Hitachi Province|ja|常陸国}}''' - {{ill|Shida Horse Pasture|ja|信太馬牧}} * '''{{ill|Shimōsa Province|ja|下総国}}''' - {{ill|Takatsu Horse Pasture|ja|高津馬牧}}, {{ill|Oiyui Horse Pasture|ja|大結馬牧}}, {{ill|Kijima Horse Pasture|ja|木島馬牧}}, {{ill|Nagasu Horse Grounds|ja|長洲馬場}}, {{ill|Ukishima Cattle Grounds|ja|浮島牛場}} * '''{{ill|Kazusa Province|ja|上総国}}''' - {{ill|Ōno Horse Pasture|ja|大野馬牧}}, {{ill|Onoya Cattle Pasture|ja|負野牛牧}}, {{ill|Araya Pasture|ja|新屋牧}} * '''{{ill|Awa Province (Japan)|ja|安房国}}''' - {{ill|Shirahama Horse Pasture|ja|白浜馬牧}}, {{ill|Kozu Horse Pasture|ja|粉師馬牧}} * '''{{ill|Sagami Province|ja|相模国}}''' - {{ill|Takano Horse and Cattle Pasture|ja|高野馬牛牧}} * '''{{ill|Suruga Province|ja|駿河国}}''' - {{ill|Okano Horse Pasture|ja|岡野馬牧}}, {{ill|Somi Horse Pasture|ja|蘇弥馬牧}} * '''{{ill|Bizen Province|ja|備前国}}''' - {{ill|Nagashima Horse and Cattle Pasture|ja|長嶋馬牛牧}}

{{redirect|Ushimaki|For the district in Moriyama Ward, Nagoya, Aichi Prefecture, see {{ill|Ushimaki (Nagoya)|ja|牛牧 (名古屋市)}}}} '''{{ill|Shokoku Maki|ja|諸国牧}}''' (しょこくまき) refers to the pastures developed and managed by the {{ill|Ministry of War (Japan, Ritsuryō system)|ja|兵部省}} in ancient Japan. These pastures primarily served as breeding grounds for {{ill|warhorses|ja|軍馬}}, horses for the aristocracy ({{ill|tribute horses|ja|貢馬}}), and {{ill|post horses|ja|伝馬}} for relay stations. Additionally, to accommodate horses and cattle gathered from the Shokoku Maki, separate pastures called '''{{ill|Kintō Maki|ja|近都牧}}''' were established in Kyushu and around the Kinai region for free-range grazing. This article covers both Shokoku Maki and Kintō Maki.
* Apart from these official pastures, the {{ill|Imperial Stables Bureau|ja|内厩寮}} also managed imperial pastures known as {{ill|Chokushi Maki|ja|勅旨牧}}.
== Overview == The exact time when the '''Shokoku Maki''' and '''Kintō Maki''' were established is unclear, but they are believed to have originated in the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}. Shokoku Maki were pastures under the jurisdiction of the {{ill|Ministry of War (Japan, Ritsuryō system)|ja|兵部省}}. According to the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}, there were 24 horse pastures in 18 provinces, 12 cattle pastures, and 3 combined horse and cattle pastures, totaling 39 official pastures. Each year, five-year-old horses from these pastures were sent to the {{ill|Left and Right Stables Bureau|ja|左右馬寮}}, while those from the pastures in the western provinces ({{ill|Saikaidō|ja|西海道}}) were sent to the {{ill|Dazaifu (Japan)|ja|大宰府}}.
The '''{{ill|Kintō Maki|ja|近都牧}}''' were pastures set up to allow these tribute horses and cattle to graze freely. These were primarily located in {{ill|Kyushu|ja|九州}} and around the {{ill|Kinai|ja|近畿}} region.
Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, each pasture was managed by: - One '''{{ill|Makichō|ja|牧長}}''' (chief overseer of the pasture), - One '''{{ill|Makichō (scribe)|ja|牧帳}}''' (scribe responsible for administrative records), - Two '''{{ill|Makiko|ja|牧子}}''' (caretakers) for every 100 horses or cattle.
The {{ill|Provincial governor|ja|国司}} supervised these officials. At the central government level, the {{ill|Hyōbashi|ja|兵馬司}} oversaw all pastures, both state-owned and private. Horses deemed suitable for riding were sent to military garrisons, where wealthier {{ill|soldiers|ja|兵士}} cared for them, while lower-quality horses were used as relay station mounts. This system ensured state control over the supply and management of cavalry horses.
If pasture officials (Makichō, Makichō scribes, and Makiko caretakers) allowed the horses to become emaciated, lost animals, or failed to meet the mandated increase of 60 foals per 100 breeding mares, they were punished. Conversely, those who exceeded the quota were rewarded with rice.
== Pastures and Locations ==
=== === Shokoku Maki (Provincial Pastures) === * '''{{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja|下野国}}''' ** {{ill|Shumon Horse Pasture|ja|朱門馬牧}} * '''{{ill|Hitachi Province|ja|常陸国}}''' ** {{ill|Shida Horse Pasture|ja|信太馬牧}} * '''{{ill|Shimōsa Province|ja|下総国}}''' ** {{ill|Takatsu Horse Pasture|ja|高津馬牧}}, {{ill|Oiyui Horse Pasture|ja|大結馬牧}}, {{ill|Kijima Horse Pasture|ja|木島馬牧}}, {{ill|Nagasu Horse Grounds|ja|長洲馬場}}, {{ill|Ukishima Cattle Grounds|ja|浮島牛場}} * '''{{ill|Kazusa Province|ja|上総国}}''' ** {{ill|Ōno Horse Pasture|ja|大野馬牧}}, {{ill|Onoya Cattle Pasture|ja|負野牛牧}}, {{ill|Araya Pasture|ja|新屋牧}} * '''{{ill|Awa Province (Japan)|ja|安房国}}''' ** {{ill|Shirahama Horse Pasture|ja|白浜馬牧}}, {{ill|Kozu Horse Pasture|ja|粉師馬牧}} * '''{{ill|Sagami Province|ja|相模国}}''' ** {{ill|Takano Horse and Cattle Pasture|ja|高野馬牛牧}} * '''{{ill|Suruga Province|ja|駿河国}}''' ** {{ill|Okano Horse Pasture|ja|岡野馬牧}}, {{ill|Somi Horse Pasture|ja|蘇弥馬牧}} * '''{{ill|Bizen Province|ja|備前国}}''' ** {{ill|Nagashima Horse and Cattle Pasture|ja|長嶋馬牛牧}}
=== === Kintō Maki (Capital-Adjacent Pastures) === * '''{{ill|Settsu Province|ja|摂津国}}''' ** {{ill|Torigai Pasture|ja|鳥養牧}}, {{ill|Toyoshima Pasture|ja|豊島牧}}, {{ill|Inano Pasture|ja|為奈野牧}} * '''{{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国}}''' ** {{ill|Kōga Pasture|ja|甲賀牧}} * '''{{ill|Tanba Province|ja|丹波国}}''' ** {{ill|Goma Pasture|ja|胡麻牧}} * '''{{ill|Harima Province|ja|播磨国}}''' ** {{ill|Tarumi Pasture|ja|垂水牧 (播磨国)}} * '''{{ill|Dazaifu (Japan)|ja|大宰府}}'''

== Overview == The exact time when the '''Shokoku Maki''' and '''Kintō Maki''' were established is unclear, but they are believed to have originated in the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}. Shokoku Maki were pastures under the jurisdiction of the {{ill|Ministry of War (Japan, Ritsuryō system)|ja|兵部省}}. According to the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}, there were 24 horse pastures in 18 provinces, 12 cattle pastures, and 3 combined horse and cattle pastures, totaling 39 official pastures. Each year, five-year-old horses from these pastures were sent to the {{ill|Left and Right Stables Bureau|ja|左右馬寮}}, while those from the pastures in the western provinces ({{ill|Saikaidō|ja|西海道}}) were sent to the {{ill|Dazaifu (Japan)|ja|大宰府}}.
The '''{{ill|Kintō Maki|ja|近都牧}}''' were pastures set up to allow these tribute horses and cattle to graze freely. These were primarily located in {{ill|Kyushu|ja|九州}} and around the {{ill|Kinai|ja|近畿}} region.
Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, each pasture was managed by: - One '''{{ill|Makichō|ja|牧長}}''' (chief overseer of the pasture), - One '''{{ill|Makichō (scribe)|ja|牧帳}}''' (scribe responsible for administrative records), - Two '''{{ill|Makiko|ja|牧子}}''' (caretakers) for every 100 horses or cattle.
The {{ill|Provincial governor|ja|国司}} supervised these officials. At the central government level, the {{ill|Hyōbashi|ja|兵馬司}} oversaw all pastures, both state-owned and private. Horses deemed suitable for riding were sent to military garrisons, where wealthier {{ill|soldiers|ja|兵士}} cared for them, while lower-quality horses were used as relay station mounts. This system ensured state control over the supply and management of cavalry horses.
If pasture officials (Makichō, Makichō scribes, and Makiko caretakers) allowed the horses to become emaciated, lost animals, or failed to meet the mandated increase of 60 foals per 100 breeding mares, they were punished. Conversely, those who exceeded the quota were rewarded with rice.

Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, each pasture was managed by: - One '''{{ill|Makichō|ja|牧長}}''' (chief overseer of the pasture), - One '''{{ill|Makichō (scribe)|ja|牧帳}}''' (scribe responsible for administrative records), - Two '''{{ill|Makiko|ja|牧子}}''' (caretakers) for every 100 horses or cattle.
The {{ill|Provincial governor|ja|国司}} supervised these officials. At the central government level, the {{ill|Hyōbashi|ja|兵馬司}} oversaw all pastures, both state-owned and private. Horses deemed suitable for riding were sent to military garrisons, where wealthier {{ill|soldiers|ja|兵士}} cared for them, while lower-quality horses were used as relay station mounts. This system ensured state control over the supply and management of cavalry horses.
If pasture officials (Makichō, Makichō scribes, and Makiko caretakers) allowed the horses to become emaciated, lost animals, or failed to meet the mandated increase of 60 foals per 100 breeding mares, they were punished. Conversely, those who exceeded the quota were rewarded with rice.

{{redirect|Ushimaki|For the district in Moriyama Ward, Nagoya, Aichi Prefecture, see {{ill|Ushimaki (Nagoya)|ja|牛牧 (名古屋市)}}}} '''{{ill|Shokoku Maki|ja|諸国牧}}''' (しょこくまき) refers to the pastures developed and managed by the {{ill|Ministry of War (Japan, Ritsuryō system)|ja|兵部省}} in ancient Japan. These pastures primarily served as breeding grounds for {{ill|warhorses|ja|軍馬}}, horses for the aristocracy ({{ill|tribute horses|ja|貢馬}}), and {{ill|post horses|ja|伝馬}} for relay stations. Additionally, to accommodate horses and cattle gathered from the Shokoku Maki, separate pastures called '''{{ill|Kintō Maki|ja|近都牧}}''' were established in Kyushu and around the Kinai region for free-range grazing. This article covers both Shokoku Maki and Kintō Maki.
* Apart from these official pastures, the {{ill|Imperial Stables Bureau|ja|内厩寮}} also managed imperial pastures known as {{ill|Chokushi Maki|ja|勅旨牧}}.
== Overview == The exact time when the '''Shokoku Maki''' and '''Kintō Maki''' were established is unclear, but they are believed to have originated in the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}. Shokoku Maki were pastures under the jurisdiction of the {{ill|Ministry of War (Japan, Ritsuryō system)|ja|兵部省}}. According to the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}, there were 24 horse pastures in 18 provinces, 12 cattle pastures, and 3 combined horse and cattle pastures, totaling 39 official pastures. Each year, five-year-old horses from these pastures were sent to the {{ill|Left and Right Stables Bureau|ja|左右馬寮}}, while those from the pastures in the western provinces ({{ill|Saikaidō|ja|西海道}}) were sent to the {{ill|Dazaifu (Japan)|ja|大宰府}}.
The '''{{ill|Kintō Maki|ja|近都牧}}''' were pastures set up to allow these tribute horses and cattle to graze freely. These were primarily located in {{ill|Kyushu|ja|九州}} and around the {{ill|Kinai|ja|近畿}} region.
Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, each pasture was managed by: - One '''{{ill|Makichō|ja|牧長}}''' (chief overseer of the pasture), - One '''{{ill|Makichō (scribe)|ja|牧帳}}''' (scribe responsible for administrative records), - Two '''{{ill|Makiko|ja|牧子}}''' (caretakers) for every 100 horses or cattle.
The {{ill|Provincial governor|ja|国司}} supervised these officials. At the central government level, the {{ill|Hyōbashi|ja|兵馬司}} oversaw all pastures, both state-owned and private. Horses deemed suitable for riding were sent to military garrisons, where wealthier {{ill|soldiers|ja|兵士}} cared for them, while lower-quality horses were used as relay station mounts. This system ensured state control over the supply and management of cavalry horses.
If pasture officials (Makichō, Makichō scribes, and Makiko caretakers) allowed the horses to become emaciated, lost animals, or failed to meet the mandated increase of 60 foals per 100 breeding mares, they were punished. Conversely, those who exceeded the quota were rewarded with rice.
== Pastures and Locations ==
=== Shokoku Maki (Provincial Pastures) === * '''{{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja|下野国}}''' ** {{ill|Shumon Horse Pasture|ja|朱門馬牧}} * '''{{ill|Hitachi Province|ja|常陸国}}''' ** {{ill|Shida Horse Pasture|ja|信太馬牧}} * '''{{ill|Shimōsa Province|ja|下総国}}''' ** {{ill|Takatsu Horse Pasture|ja|高津馬牧}}, {{ill|Oiyui Horse Pasture|ja|大結馬牧}}, {{ill|Kijima Horse Pasture|ja|木島馬牧}}, {{ill|Nagasu Horse Grounds|ja|長洲馬場}}, {{ill|Ukishima Cattle Grounds|ja|浮島牛場}} * '''{{ill|Kazusa Province|ja|上総国}}''' ** {{ill|Ōno Horse Pasture|ja|大野馬牧}}, {{ill|Onoya Cattle Pasture|ja|負野牛牧}}, {{ill|Araya Pasture|ja|新屋牧}} * '''{{ill|Awa Province (Japan)|ja|安房国}}''' ** {{ill|Shirahama Horse Pasture|ja|白浜馬牧}}, {{ill|Kozu Horse Pasture|ja|粉師馬牧}} * '''{{ill|Sagami Province|ja|相模国}}''' ** {{ill|Takano Horse and Cattle Pasture|ja|高野馬牛牧}} * '''{{ill|Suruga Province|ja|駿河国}}''' ** {{ill|Okano Horse Pasture|ja|岡野馬牧}}, {{ill|Somi Horse Pasture|ja|蘇弥馬牧}} * '''{{ill|Bizen Province|ja|備前国}}''' ** {{ill|Nagashima Horse and Cattle Pasture|ja|長嶋馬牛牧}}
=== Kintō Maki (Capital-Adjacent Pastures) === * '''{{ill|Settsu Province|ja|摂津国}}''' ** {{ill|Torigai Pasture|ja|鳥養牧}}, {{ill|Toyoshima Pasture|ja|豊島牧}}, {{ill|Inano Pasture|ja|為奈野牧}} * '''{{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国}}''' ** {{ill|Kōga Pasture|ja|甲賀牧}} * '''{{ill|Tanba Province|ja|丹波国}}''' ** {{ill|Goma Pasture|ja|胡麻牧}} * '''{{ill|Harima Province|ja|播磨国}}''' ** {{ill|Tarumi Pasture|ja|垂水牧 (播磨国)}} * '''{{ill|Dazaifu (government)|lt=Dazaifu|ja|大宰府}}'''
== See Also == * {{ill|Chokushi Maki|ja|勅旨牧}}

revision.text:
{{redirect|Ushimaki|For the district in Moriyama Ward, Nagoya, Aichi Prefecture, see {{ill|Ushimaki (Nagoya)|ja|牛牧 (名古屋市)}}}} '''{{ill|Shokoku Maki|ja|諸国牧}}''' (しょこくまき) refers to the pastures developed and managed by the {{ill|Ministry of War (Japan, Ritsuryō system)|ja|兵部省}} in ancient Japan. These pastures primarily served as breeding grounds for {{ill|warhorses|ja|軍馬}}, horses for the aristocracy ({{ill|tribute horses|ja|貢馬}}), and {{ill|post horses|ja|伝馬}} for relay stations. Additionally, to accommodate horses and cattle gathered from the Shokoku Maki, separate pastures called '''{{ill|Kintō Maki|ja|近都牧}}''' were established in Kyushu and around the Kinai region for free-range grazing. This article covers both Shokoku Maki and Kintō Maki.
* Apart from these official pastures, the {{ill|Imperial Stables Bureau|ja|内厩寮}} also managed imperial pastures known as {{ill|Chokushi Maki|ja|勅旨牧}}.
== Overview == The exact time when the '''Shokoku Maki''' and '''Kintō Maki''' were established is unclear, but they are believed to have originated in the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}. Shokoku Maki were pastures under the jurisdiction of the {{ill|Ministry of War (Japan, Ritsuryō system)|ja|兵部省}}. According to the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}, there were 24 horse pastures in 18 provinces, 12 cattle pastures, and 3 combined horse and cattle pastures, totaling 39 official pastures. Each year, five-year-old horses from these pastures were sent to the {{ill|Left and Right Stables Bureau|ja|左右馬寮}}, while those from the pastures in the western provinces ({{ill|Saikaidō|ja|西海道}}) were sent to the {{ill|Dazaifu (Japan)|ja|大宰府}}.
The '''{{ill|Kintō Maki|ja|近都牧}}''' were pastures set up to allow these tribute horses and cattle to graze freely. These were primarily located in {{ill|Kyushu|ja|九州}} and around the {{ill|Kinai|ja|近畿}} region.
Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, each pasture was managed by: - One '''{{ill|Makichō|ja|牧長}}''' (chief overseer of the pasture), - One '''{{ill|Makichō (scribe)|ja|牧帳}}''' (scribe responsible for administrative records), - Two '''{{ill|Makiko|ja|牧子}}''' (caretakers) for every 100 horses or cattle.
The {{ill|Provincial governor|ja|国司}} supervised these officials. At the central government level, the {{ill|Hyōbashi|ja|兵馬司}} oversaw all pastures, both state-owned and private. Horses deemed suitable for riding were sent to military garrisons, where wealthier {{ill|soldiers|ja|兵士}} cared for them, while lower-quality horses were used as relay station mounts. This system ensured state control over the supply and management of cavalry horses.
If pasture officials (Makichō, Makichō scribes, and Makiko caretakers) allowed the horses to become emaciated, lost animals, or failed to meet the mandated increase of 60 foals per 100 breeding mares, they were punished. Conversely, those who exceeded the quota were rewarded with rice.
== Pastures and Locations ==
=== Shokoku Maki (Provincial Pastures) === * '''{{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja|下野国}}''' ** {{ill|Shumon Horse Pasture|ja|朱門馬牧}} * '''{{ill|Hitachi Province|ja|常陸国}}''' ** {{ill|Shida Horse Pasture|ja|信太馬牧}} * '''{{ill|Shimōsa Province|ja|下総国}}''' ** {{ill|Takatsu Horse Pasture|ja|高津馬牧}}, {{ill|Oiyui Horse Pasture|ja|大結馬牧}}, {{ill|Kijima Horse Pasture|ja|木島馬牧}}, {{ill|Nagasu Horse Grounds|ja|長洲馬場}}, {{ill|Ukishima Cattle Grounds|ja|浮島牛場}} * '''{{ill|Kazusa Province|ja|上総国}}''' ** {{ill|Ōno Horse Pasture|ja|大野馬牧}}, {{ill|Onoya Cattle Pasture|ja|負野牛牧}}, {{ill|Araya Pasture|ja|新屋牧}} * '''{{ill|Awa Province (Japan)|ja|安房国}}''' ** {{ill|Shirahama Horse Pasture|ja|白浜馬牧}}, {{ill|Kozu Horse Pasture|ja|粉師馬牧}} * '''{{ill|Sagami Province|ja|相模国}}''' ** {{ill|Takano Horse and Cattle Pasture|ja|高野馬牛牧}} * '''{{ill|Suruga Province|ja|駿河国}}''' ** {{ill|Okano Horse Pasture|ja|岡野馬牧}}, {{ill|Somi Horse Pasture|ja|蘇弥馬牧}} * '''{{ill|Bizen Province|ja|備前国}}''' ** {{ill|Nagashima Horse and Cattle Pasture|ja|長嶋馬牛牧}}
=== Kintō Maki (Capital-Adjacent Pastures) === * '''{{ill|Settsu Province|ja|摂津国}}''' ** {{ill|Torigai Pasture|ja|鳥養牧}}, {{ill|Toyoshima Pasture|ja|豊島牧}}, {{ill|Inano Pasture|ja|為奈野牧}} * '''{{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国}}''' ** {{ill|Kōga Pasture|ja|甲賀牧}} * '''{{ill|Tanba Province|ja|丹波国}}''' ** {{ill|Goma Pasture|ja|胡麻牧}} * '''{{ill|Harima Province|ja|播磨国}}''' ** {{ill|Tarumi Pasture|ja|垂水牧 (播磨国)}} * '''{{ill|Dazaifu (government)|lt=Dazaifu|ja|大宰府}}'''
== See Also == * {{ill|Chokushi Maki|ja|勅旨牧}}

Each year, five-year-old horses from these pastures were sent to the {{ill|Left and Right Stables Bureau|ja|左右馬寮}}, while those from the pastures in the western provinces ({{ill|Saikaidō|ja|西海道}}) were sent to the {{ill|Dazaifu (Japan)|ja|大宰府}}.
The '''{{ill|Kintō Maki|ja|近都牧}}''' were pastures set up to allow these tribute horses and cattle to graze freely. These were primarily located in {{ill|Kyushu|ja|九州}} and around the {{ill|Kinai|ja|近畿}} region.
Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, each pasture was managed by: - One '''{{ill|Makichō|ja|牧長}}''' (chief overseer of the pasture), - One '''{{ill|Makichō (scribe)|ja|牧帳}}''' (scribe responsible for administrative records), - Two '''{{ill|Makiko|ja|牧子}}''' (caretakers) for every 100 horses or cattle.
The {{ill|Provincial governor|ja|国司}} supervised these officials. At the central government level, the {{ill|Hyōbashi|ja|兵馬司}} oversaw all pastures, both state-owned and private. Horses deemed suitable for riding were sent to military garrisons, where wealthier {{ill|soldiers|ja|兵士}} cared for them, while lower-quality horses were used as relay station mounts. This system ensured state control over the supply and management of cavalry horses.
If pasture officials (Makichō, Makichō scribes, and Makiko caretakers) allowed the horses to become emaciated, lost animals, or failed to meet the mandated increase of 60 foals per 100 breeding mares, they were punished. Conversely, those who exceeded the quota were rewarded with rice.

The {{ill|Provincial governor|ja|国司}} supervised these officials. At the central government level, the {{ill|Hyōbashi|ja|兵馬司}} oversaw all pastures, both state-owned and private. Horses deemed suitable for riding were sent to military garrisons, where wealthier {{ill|soldiers|ja|兵士}} cared for them, while lower-quality horses were used as relay station mounts. This system ensured state control over the supply and management of cavalry horses.
If pasture officials (Makichō, Makichō scribes, and Makiko caretakers) allowed the horses to become emaciated, lost animals, or failed to meet the mandated increase of 60 foals per 100 breeding mares, they were punished. Conversely, those who exceeded the quota were rewarded with rice.

{{redirect|Ushimaki|For the district in Moriyama Ward, Nagoya, Aichi Prefecture, see {{ill|Ushimaki (Nagoya)|ja|牛牧 (名古屋市)}}}} '''{{ill|Shokoku Maki|ja|諸国牧}}''' (しょこくまき) refers to the pastures developed and managed by the {{ill|Ministry of War (Japan, Ritsuryō system)|ja|兵部省}} in ancient Japan. These pastures primarily served as breeding grounds for {{ill|warhorses|ja|軍馬}}, horses for the aristocracy ({{ill|tribute horses|ja|貢馬}}), and {{ill|post horses|ja|伝馬}} for relay stations. Additionally, to accommodate horses and cattle gathered from the Shokoku Maki, separate pastures called '''{{ill|Kintō Maki|ja|近都牧}}''' were established in Kyushu and around the Kinai region for free-range grazing. This article covers both Shokoku Maki and Kintō Maki.
* Apart from these official pastures, the {{ill|Imperial Stables Bureau|ja|内厩寮}} also managed imperial pastures known as {{ill|Chokushi Maki|ja|勅旨牧}}.
== Overview == The exact time when the '''Shokoku Maki''' and '''Kintō Maki''' were established is unclear, but they are believed to have originated in the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}. Shokoku Maki were pastures under the jurisdiction of the {{ill|Ministry of War (Japan, Ritsuryō system)|ja|兵部省}}. According to the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}, there were 24 horse pastures in 18 provinces, 12 cattle pastures, and 3 combined horse and cattle pastures, totaling 39 official pastures. Each year, five-year-old horses from these pastures were sent to the {{ill|Left and Right Stables Bureau|ja|左右馬寮}}, while those from the pastures in the western provinces ({{ill|Saikaidō|ja|西海道}}) were sent to the {{ill|Dazaifu (Japan)|ja|大宰府}}.
The '''{{ill|Kintō Maki|ja|近都牧}}''' were pastures set up to allow these tribute horses and cattle to graze freely. These were primarily located in {{ill|Kyushu|ja|九州}} and around the {{ill|Kinai|ja|近畿}} region.
Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, each pasture was managed by: - One '''{{ill|Makichō|ja|牧長}}''' (chief overseer of the pasture), - One '''{{ill|Makichō (scribe)|ja|牧帳}}''' (scribe responsible for administrative records), - Two '''{{ill|Makiko|ja|牧子}}''' (caretakers) for every 100 horses or cattle.
The {{ill|Provincial governor|ja|国司}} supervised these officials. At the central government level, the {{ill|Hyōbashi|ja|兵馬司}} oversaw all pastures, both state-owned and private. Horses deemed suitable for riding were sent to military garrisons, where wealthier {{ill|soldiers|ja|兵士}} cared for them, while lower-quality horses were used as relay station mounts. This system ensured state control over the supply and management of cavalry horses.
If pasture officials (Makichō, Makichō scribes, and Makiko caretakers) allowed the horses to become emaciated, lost animals, or failed to meet the mandated increase of 60 foals per 100 breeding mares, they were punished. Conversely, those who exceeded the quota were rewarded with rice.
== Pastures and Locations ==
=== Shokoku Maki (Provincial Pastures) === * '''{{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja|下野国}}''' ** {{ill|Shumon Horse Pasture|ja|朱門馬牧}} * '''{{ill|Hitachi Province|ja|常陸国}}''' ** {{ill|Shida Horse Pasture|ja|信太馬牧}} * '''{{ill|Shimōsa Province|ja|下総国}}''' ** {{ill|Takatsu Horse Pasture|ja|高津馬牧}}, {{ill|Oiyui Horse Pasture|ja|大結馬牧}}, {{ill|Kijima Horse Pasture|ja|木島馬牧}}, {{ill|Nagasu Horse Grounds|ja|長洲馬場}}, {{ill|Ukishima Cattle Grounds|ja|浮島牛場}} * '''{{ill|Kazusa Province|ja|上総国}}''' ** {{ill|Ōno Horse Pasture|ja|大野馬牧}}, {{ill|Onoya Cattle Pasture|ja|負野牛牧}}, {{ill|Araya Pasture|ja|新屋牧}} * '''{{ill|Awa Province (Japan)|ja|安房国}}''' ** {{ill|Shirahama Horse Pasture|ja|白浜馬牧}}, {{ill|Kozu Horse Pasture|ja|粉師馬牧}} * '''{{ill|Sagami Province|ja|相模国}}''' ** {{ill|Takano Horse and Cattle Pasture|ja|高野馬牛牧}} * '''{{ill|Suruga Province|ja|駿河国}}''' ** {{ill|Okano Horse Pasture|ja|岡野馬牧}}, {{ill|Somi Horse Pasture|ja|蘇弥馬牧}} * '''{{ill|Bizen Province|ja|備前国}}''' ** {{ill|Nagashima Horse and Cattle Pasture|ja|長嶋馬牛牧}}
=== Kintō Maki (Capital-Adjacent Pastures) === * '''{{ill|Settsu Province|ja|摂津国}}''' ** {{ill|Torigai Pasture|ja|鳥養牧}}, {{ill|Toyoshima Pasture|ja|豊島牧}}, {{ill|Inano Pasture|ja|為奈野牧}} * '''{{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国}}''' ** {{ill|Kōga Pasture|ja|甲賀牧}} * '''{{ill|Tanba Province|ja|丹波国}}''' ** {{ill|Goma Pasture|ja|胡麻牧}} * '''{{ill|Harima Province|ja|播磨国}}''' ** {{ill|Tarumi Pasture|ja|垂水牧 (播磨国)}} * '''{{ill|Dazaifu (government)|lt=Dazaifu|ja|大宰府}}'''
== See Also == * {{ill|Chokushi Maki|ja|勅旨牧}}

revision.text:
{{redirect|Ushimaki|For the district in Moriyama Ward, Nagoya, Aichi Prefecture, see {{ill|Ushimaki (Nagoya)|ja|牛牧 (名古屋市)}}}} '''{{ill|Shokoku Maki|ja|諸国牧}}''' (しょこくまき) refers to the pastures developed and managed by the {{ill|Ministry of War (Japan, Ritsuryō system)|ja|兵部省}} in ancient Japan. These pastures primarily served as breeding grounds for {{ill|warhorses|ja|軍馬}}, horses for the aristocracy ({{ill|tribute horses|ja|貢馬}}), and {{ill|post horses|ja|伝馬}} for relay stations. Additionally, to accommodate horses and cattle gathered from the Shokoku Maki, separate pastures called '''{{ill|Kintō Maki|ja|近都牧}}''' were established in Kyushu and around the Kinai region for free-range grazing. This article covers both Shokoku Maki and Kintō Maki.
* Apart from these official pastures, the {{ill|Imperial Stables Bureau|ja|内厩寮}} also managed imperial pastures known as {{ill|Chokushi Maki|ja|勅旨牧}}.
== Overview == The exact time when the '''Shokoku Maki''' and '''Kintō Maki''' were established is unclear, but they are believed to have originated in the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}. Shokoku Maki were pastures under the jurisdiction of the {{ill|Ministry of War (Japan, Ritsuryō system)|ja|兵部省}}. According to the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}, there were 24 horse pastures in 18 provinces, 12 cattle pastures, and 3 combined horse and cattle pastures, totaling 39 official pastures. Each year, five-year-old horses from these pastures were sent to the {{ill|Left and Right Stables Bureau|ja|左右馬寮}}, while those from the pastures in the western provinces ({{ill|Saikaidō|ja|西海道}}) were sent to the {{ill|Dazaifu (Japan)|ja|大宰府}}.
The '''{{ill|Kintō Maki|ja|近都牧}}''' were pastures set up to allow these tribute horses and cattle to graze freely. These were primarily located in {{ill|Kyushu|ja|九州}} and around the {{ill|Kinai|ja|近畿}} region.
Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, each pasture was managed by: - One '''{{ill|Makichō|ja|牧長}}''' (chief overseer of the pasture), - One '''{{ill|Makichō (scribe)|ja|牧帳}}''' (scribe responsible for administrative records), - Two '''{{ill|Makiko|ja|牧子}}''' (caretakers) for every 100 horses or cattle.
The {{ill|Provincial governor|ja|国司}} supervised these officials. At the central government level, the {{ill|Hyōbashi|ja|兵馬司}} oversaw all pastures, both state-owned and private. Horses deemed suitable for riding were sent to military garrisons, where wealthier {{ill|soldiers|ja|兵士}} cared for them, while lower-quality horses were used as relay station mounts. This system ensured state control over the supply and management of cavalry horses.
If pasture officials (Makichō, Makichō scribes, and Makiko caretakers) allowed the horses to become emaciated, lost animals, or failed to meet the mandated increase of 60 foals per 100 breeding mares, they were punished. Conversely, those who exceeded the quota were rewarded with rice.
== Pastures and Locations ==
=== Shokoku Maki (Provincial Pastures) === * '''{{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja|下野国}}''' ** {{ill|Shumon Horse Pasture|ja|朱門馬牧}} * '''{{ill|Hitachi Province|ja|常陸国}}''' ** {{ill|Shida Horse Pasture|ja|信太馬牧}} * '''{{ill|Shimōsa Province|ja|下総国}}''' ** {{ill|Takatsu Horse Pasture|ja|高津馬牧}}, {{ill|Oiyui Horse Pasture|ja|大結馬牧}}, {{ill|Kijima Horse Pasture|ja|木島馬牧}}, {{ill|Nagasu Horse Grounds|ja|長洲馬場}}, {{ill|Ukishima Cattle Grounds|ja|浮島牛場}} * '''{{ill|Kazusa Province|ja|上総国}}''' ** {{ill|Ōno Horse Pasture|ja|大野馬牧}}, {{ill|Onoya Cattle Pasture|ja|負野牛牧}}, {{ill|Araya Pasture|ja|新屋牧}} * '''{{ill|Awa Province (Japan)|ja|安房国}}''' ** {{ill|Shirahama Horse Pasture|ja|白浜馬牧}}, {{ill|Kozu Horse Pasture|ja|粉師馬牧}} * '''{{ill|Sagami Province|ja|相模国}}''' ** {{ill|Takano Horse and Cattle Pasture|ja|高野馬牛牧}} * '''{{ill|Suruga Province|ja|駿河国}}''' ** {{ill|Okano Horse Pasture|ja|岡野馬牧}}, {{ill|Somi Horse Pasture|ja|蘇弥馬牧}} * '''{{ill|Bizen Province|ja|備前国}}''' ** {{ill|Nagashima Horse and Cattle Pasture|ja|長嶋馬牛牧}}
=== Kintō Maki (Capital-Adjacent Pastures) === * '''{{ill|Settsu Province|ja|摂津国}}''' ** {{ill|Torigai Pasture|ja|鳥養牧}}, {{ill|Toyoshima Pasture|ja|豊島牧}}, {{ill|Inano Pasture|ja|為奈野牧}} * '''{{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国}}''' ** {{ill|Kōga Pasture|ja|甲賀牧}} * '''{{ill|Tanba Province|ja|丹波国}}''' ** {{ill|Goma Pasture|ja|胡麻牧}} * '''{{ill|Harima Province|ja|播磨国}}''' ** {{ill|Tarumi Pasture|ja|垂水牧 (播磨国)}} * '''{{ill|Dazaifu (government)|lt=Dazaifu|ja|大宰府}}''' == See Also == * {{ill|Chokushi Maki|ja|勅旨牧}}

* Apart from these official pastures, the {{ill|Imperial Stables Bureau|ja|内厩寮}} also managed imperial pastures known as {{ill|Chokushi Maki|ja|勅旨牧}}.
== Overview == The exact time when the '''Shokoku Maki''' and '''Kintō Maki''' were established is unclear, but they are believed to have originated in the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}. Shokoku Maki were pastures under the jurisdiction of the {{ill|Ministry of War (Japan, Ritsuryō system)|ja|兵部省}}. According to the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}, there were 24 horse pastures in 18 provinces, 12 cattle pastures, and 3 combined horse and cattle pastures, totaling 39 official pastures. Each year, five-year-old horses from these pastures were sent to the {{ill|Left and Right Stables Bureau|ja|左右馬寮}}, while those from the pastures in the western provinces ({{ill|Saikaidō|ja|西海道}}) were sent to the {{ill|Dazaifu (Japan)|ja|大宰府}}.
The '''{{ill|Kintō Maki|ja|近都牧}}''' were pastures set up to allow these tribute horses and cattle to graze freely. These were primarily located in {{ill|Kyushu|ja|九州}} and around the {{ill|Kinai|ja|近畿}} region.
Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, each pasture was managed by: - One '''{{ill|Makichō|ja|牧長}}''' (chief overseer of the pasture), - One '''{{ill|Makichō (scribe)|ja|牧帳}}''' (scribe responsible for administrative records), - Two '''{{ill|Makiko|ja|牧子}}''' (caretakers) for every 100 horses or cattle.
The {{ill|Provincial governor|ja|国司}} supervised these officials. At the central government level, the {{ill|Hyōbashi|ja|兵馬司}} oversaw all pastures, both state-owned and private. Horses deemed suitable for riding were sent to military garrisons, where wealthier {{ill|soldiers|ja|兵士}} cared for them, while lower-quality horses were used as relay station mounts. This system ensured state control over the supply and management of cavalry horses.
If pasture officials (Makichō, Makichō scribes, and Makiko caretakers) allowed the horses to become emaciated, lost animals, or failed to meet the mandated increase of 60 foals per 100 breeding mares, they were punished. Conversely, those who exceeded the quota were rewarded with rice.
== Pastures and Locations ==
=== Shokoku Maki (Provincial Pastures) === * '''{{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja|下野国}}''' ** {{ill|Shumon Horse Pasture|ja|朱門馬牧}} * '''{{ill|Hitachi Province|ja|常陸国}}''' ** {{ill|Shida Horse Pasture|ja|信太馬牧}} * '''{{ill|Shimōsa Province|ja|下総国}}''' ** {{ill|Takatsu Horse Pasture|ja|高津馬牧}}, {{ill|Oiyui Horse Pasture|ja|大結馬牧}}, {{ill|Kijima Horse Pasture|ja|木島馬牧}}, {{ill|Nagasu Horse Grounds|ja|長洲馬場}}, {{ill|Ukishima Cattle Grounds|ja|浮島牛場}} * '''{{ill|Kazusa Province|ja|上総国}}''' ** {{ill|Ōno Horse Pasture|ja|大野馬牧}}, {{ill|Onoya Cattle Pasture|ja|負野牛牧}}, {{ill|Araya Pasture|ja|新屋牧}} * '''{{ill|Awa Province (Japan)|ja|安房国}}''' ** {{ill|Shirahama Horse Pasture|ja|白浜馬牧}}, {{ill|Kozu Horse Pasture|ja|粉師馬牧}} * '''{{ill|Sagami Province|ja|相模国}}''' ** {{ill|Takano Horse and Cattle Pasture|ja|高野馬牛牧}} * '''{{ill|Suruga Province|ja|駿河国}}''' ** {{ill|Okano Horse Pasture|ja|岡野馬牧}}, {{ill|Somi Horse Pasture|ja|蘇弥馬牧}} * '''{{ill|Bizen Province|ja|備前国}}''' ** {{ill|Nagashima Horse and Cattle Pasture|ja|長嶋馬牛牧}}
=== Kintō Maki (Capital-Adjacent Pastures) === * '''{{ill|Settsu Province|ja|摂津国}}''' ** {{ill|Torigai Pasture|ja|鳥養牧}}, {{ill|Toyoshima Pasture|ja|豊島牧}}, {{ill|Inano Pasture|ja|為奈野牧}} * '''{{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国}}''' ** {{ill|Kōga Pasture|ja|甲賀牧}} * '''{{ill|Tanba Province|ja|丹波国}}''' ** {{ill|Goma Pasture|ja|胡麻牧}} * '''{{ill|Harima Province|ja|播磨国}}''' ** {{ill|Tarumi Pasture|ja|垂水牧 (播磨国)}} * '''{{ill|Dazaifu (government)|lt=Dazaifu|ja|大宰府}}''' == See Also == * {{ill|Chokushi Maki|ja|勅旨牧}}

revision.text:
{{redirect|牛牧|愛知県名古屋市守山区の地名|牛牧 (名古屋市)}} '''諸国牧'''（しょこくまき）は、[[古代]]の日本において、[[兵部省]]により開発・管轄された[[牧場]]であり、主に[[軍馬]]や[[貴族]]への[[貢馬]]、[[伝馬|駅伝馬]]などの供給源とされた。また、諸国牧から集められた馬牛を放し飼いにするため、'''近都牧'''（きんとまき）と呼ばれる牧が九州や畿内周辺に設置された。本稿では、諸国牧と近都牧について記す。 *官牧には、ほかに[[内厩寮]]が経営していた[[勅旨牧]]がある。
== 概要 == '''諸国牧'''・'''近都牧'''がいつ設置されたのかは定かではないが、[[飛鳥時代]]の頃だと考えられている。 諸国牧は、[[兵部省]]が所轄する牧で、[[延喜式]]によると18ヵ国に馬牧が24ヵ所、牛牧が12ヵ所、馬牛牧が3ヵ所の計39の牧が設置され、毎年、各牧から馬は5歳に達したものが左右馬寮に送られ、西海道諸国の牧からは大宰府に送られた。この諸国から貢上された馬牛を放し飼いにする為に設けられたのが[[近都牧]]で、主に[[九州]]・[[近畿]]周辺に設置された。
律令制下においては、各牧には、牧の責任者の'''[[牧長]]'''（ぼくちょう）1名、文書事務にあたる'''[[牧帳]]'''（まきちょう）1名がおかれ、[[ウマ|馬]][[ウシ|牛]]100頭ごとに'''[[牧子]]'''（ぼくし）二名で飼育にあたり、[[国司]]が牧長以下らを指揮・監督していた。中央では、[[兵馬司]]が、全国の牧と公私の馬牧を管理し、牧で乗馬に適するものは、軍団に送り裕福な[[兵士]]の家で飼育され、駄馬は、駅伝場として使用されるなど、国家によって騎馬の供給管理を統制するための仕組みがつくられていた。なお、牧長・牧帳・牧子らが牧の経営にあたり、放牧中の馬を痩せさせたり、失ったり、あるいは、母蓄100頭に対して駒犢60頭という規定の増産数に達しない場合は、罰せられ、規定以上に駒犢を増やすことができれば、褒美として稲が与えられた。
==牧と所在地== === 諸国牧 === *[[下野国]] **[[朱門馬牧]] *[[常陸国]] **[[信太馬牧]] *[[下総国]] **[[高津馬牧]]、[[大結馬牧]]、[[木島馬牧]]、[[長洲馬場]]、[[浮島牛場]] *[[上総国]] **[[大野馬牧]]、[[負野牛牧]] [[新屋牧]] *[[安房国]] **[[白浜馬牧]]、[[粉師馬牧]] *[[相模国]] **[[高野馬牛牧]] *[[駿河国]] **[[岡野馬牧]]、[[蘇弥馬牧]] *[[備前国]] **[[長嶋馬牛牧]]
===近都牧=== *[[摂津国]] **[[鳥養牧]]、[[豊島牧]]、[[為奈野牧]] *[[近江国]] **[[甲賀牧]] *[[丹波国]] **[[胡麻牧]] *[[播磨国]] **[[垂水牧 (播磨国)|垂水牧]] *[[大宰府]]
== 関連項目 == *[[勅旨牧]]
{{Japanese-history-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:しよこくまき}} [[Category:日本の律令制]] [[Category:日本の牧場|歴しよこくまき]]

==牧と所在地== === 諸国牧 === *[[下野国]] **[[朱門馬牧]] *[[常陸国]] **[[信太馬牧]] *[[下総国]] **[[高津馬牧]]、[[大結馬牧]]、[[木島馬牧]]、[[長洲馬場]]、[[浮島牛場]] *[[上総国]] **[[大野馬牧]]、[[負野牛牧]] [[新屋牧]] *[[安房国]] **[[白浜馬牧]]、[[粉師馬牧]] *[[相模国]] **[[高野馬牛牧]] *[[駿河国]] **[[岡野馬牧]]、[[蘇弥馬牧]] *[[備前国]] **[[長嶋馬牛牧]]
===近都牧=== *[[摂津国]] **[[鳥養牧]]、[[豊島牧]]、[[為奈野牧]] *[[近江国]] **[[甲賀牧]] *[[丹波国]] **[[胡麻牧]] *[[播磨国]] **[[垂水牧 (播磨国)|垂水牧]] *[[大宰府]]
== 関連項目 == *[[勅旨牧]]

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q11632062]]
Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:28, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}}

{{redirect|Ushimaki|For the district in Moriyama Ward, Nagoya, Aichi Prefecture, see {{ill|Ushimaki (Nagoya)|ja|牛牧 (名古屋市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ushimaki (Nagoya)|qq=|1=Ushimaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q97218216}}}}  
'''{{ill|Shokoku Maki|ja|諸国牧|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shokoku Maki|qq=}}''' (しょこくまき) refers to the pastures developed and managed by the [[:en:Ministry of War (pre-modern Japan)|Ministry of War]] in ancient Japan. These pastures primarily served as breeding grounds for [[:en:Horses in warfare|warhorses]], horses for the aristocracy ({{ill|tribute horses|ja|貢馬|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tribute horses|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), and {{ill|post horses|ja|伝馬|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/post horses|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 駅伝制|1=post-horses|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11381453}} for relay stations. Additionally, to accommodate horses and cattle gathered from the Shokoku Maki, separate pastures called '''{{ill|Kintō Maki|ja|近都牧|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kintō Maki|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 諸国牧|1=Shokoku Maki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11632062}}''' were established in Kyushu and around the Kinai region for free-range grazing. This article covers both Shokoku Maki and Kintō Maki.

* Apart from these official pastures, the {{ill|Imperial Stables Bureau|ja|内厩寮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Stables Bureau|qq=|1=Imperial Stables Bureau|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q60985176}} also managed imperial pastures known as {{ill|Chokushi Maki|ja|勅旨牧|1=Chokushi Maki|2=ja|3=勅旨牧|4=zh|5=勅旨牧|6=fr|7=Chokushi Maki|8=de|9=Chokushi Maki|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chokushi Maki|13=User:Immanuelle/Chokushi Maki|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11399928}}.

== Overview ==
The exact time when the '''Shokoku Maki''' and '''Kintō Maki''' were established is unclear, but they are believed to have originated in the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]].  
Shokoku Maki were pastures under the jurisdiction of the [[:en:Ministry of War (pre-modern Japan)|Ministry of War]]. According to the [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]], there were 24 horse pastures in 18 provinces, 12 cattle pastures, and 3 combined horse and cattle pastures, totaling 39 official pastures. Each year, five-year-old horses from these pastures were sent to the {{ill|Left and Right Stables Bureau|ja|左右馬寮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Left and Right Stables Bureau|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, while those from the pastures in the western provinces ([[:en:Saikaidō|Saikaidō]]) were sent to the [[:en:Dazaifu (government)|Dazaifu (Japan)]].  

The '''{{ill|Kintō Maki|ja|近都牧|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kintō Maki|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 諸国牧|1=Shokoku Maki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11632062}}''' were pastures set up to allow these tribute horses and cattle to graze freely. These were primarily located in [[:en:Kyushu|Kyushu]] and around the {{ill|Kinai|ja|近畿|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinai|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近畿地方|1=Kansai region|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q164256}} region.

Under the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]], each pasture was managed by:
- One '''{{ill|Makichō|ja|牧長|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makichō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}''' (chief overseer of the pasture),
- One '''{{ill|Makichō (scribe)|ja|牧帳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makichō (scribe)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}''' (scribe responsible for administrative records),
- Two '''{{ill|Makiko|ja|牧子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makiko|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}''' (caretakers) for every 100 horses or cattle.

The [[:en:Kokushi (official)|Provincial governor]] supervised these officials.  
At the central government level, the {{ill|Hyōbashi|ja|兵馬司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyōbashi|qq=|1=Hyōbashi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11393722}} oversaw all pastures, both state-owned and private. Horses deemed suitable for riding were sent to military garrisons, where wealthier [[:en:Soldier|soldiers]] cared for them, while lower-quality horses were used as relay station mounts. This system ensured state control over the supply and management of cavalry horses.  

If pasture officials (Makichō, Makichō scribes, and Makiko caretakers) allowed the horses to become emaciated, lost animals, or failed to meet the mandated increase of 60 foals per 100 breeding mares, they were punished. Conversely, those who exceeded the quota were rewarded with rice.

== Pastures and Locations ==

=== Shokoku Maki (Provincial Pastures) ===
* '''[[:en:Shimotsuke Province|Shimotsuke Province]]'''  
** {{ill|Shumon Horse Pasture|ja|朱門馬牧|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shumon Horse Pasture|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* '''[[:en:Hitachi Province|Hitachi Province]]'''  
** {{ill|Shida Horse Pasture|ja|信太馬牧|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shida Horse Pasture|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* '''[[:en:Shimōsa Province|Shimōsa Province]]'''  
** {{ill|Takatsu Horse Pasture|ja|高津馬牧|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takatsu Horse Pasture|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Oiyui Horse Pasture|ja|大結馬牧|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oiyui Horse Pasture|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 夏見御厨|1=Oiyui Horse Pasture|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11430131}}, {{ill|Kijima Horse Pasture|ja|木島馬牧|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kijima Horse Pasture|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Nagasu Horse Grounds|ja|長洲馬場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagasu Horse Grounds|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Ukishima Cattle Grounds|ja|浮島牛場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukishima Cattle Grounds|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* '''[[:en:Kazusa Province|Kazusa Province]]'''  
** {{ill|Ōno Horse Pasture|ja|大野馬牧|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōno Horse Pasture|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Onoya Cattle Pasture|ja|負野牛牧|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onoya Cattle Pasture|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Araya Pasture|ja|新屋牧|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Araya Pasture|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* '''[[:en:Awa Province (Chiba)|Awa Province (Japan)]]'''  
** {{ill|Shirahama Horse Pasture|ja|白浜馬牧|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirahama Horse Pasture|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Kozu Horse Pasture|ja|粉師馬牧|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kozu Horse Pasture|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* '''[[:en:Sagami Province|Sagami Province]]'''

{{redirect|Ushimaki|For the district in Moriyama Ward, Nagoya, Aichi Prefecture, see {{ill|Ushimaki (Nagoya)|ja|牛牧 (名古屋市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ushimaki (Nagoya)|qq=|1=Ushimaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q97218216}}}}  
'''{{ill|Shokoku Maki|ja|諸国牧|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shokoku Maki|qq=}}''' (しょこくまき) refers to the pastures developed and managed by the [[:en:Ministry of War (pre-modern Japan)|Ministry of War]] in ancient Japan. These pastures primarily served as breeding grounds for [[:en:Horses in warfare|warhorses]], horses for the aristocracy ({{ill|tribute horses|ja|貢馬|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tribute horses|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), and {{ill|post horses|ja|伝馬|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/post horses|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 駅伝制|1=post-horses|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11381453}} for relay stations. Additionally, to accommodate horses and cattle gathered from the Shokoku Maki, separate pastures called '''{{ill|Kintō Maki|ja|近都牧|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kintō Maki|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 諸国牧|1=Shokoku Maki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11632062}}''' were established in Kyushu and around the Kinai region for free-range grazing. This article covers both Shokoku Maki and Kintō Maki.

* Apart from these official pastures, the {{ill|Imperial Stables Bureau|ja|内厩寮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Stables Bureau|qq=|1=Imperial Stables Bureau|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q60985176}} also managed imperial pastures known as {{ill|Chokushi Maki|ja|勅旨牧|1=Chokushi Maki|2=ja|3=勅旨牧|4=zh|5=勅旨牧|6=fr|7=Chokushi Maki|8=de|9=Chokushi Maki|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chokushi Maki|13=User:Immanuelle/Chokushi Maki|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11399928}}.

== Overview ==
The exact time when the '''Shokoku Maki''' and '''Kintō Maki''' were established is unclear, but they are believed to have originated in the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]].  
Shokoku Maki were pastures under the jurisdiction of the [[:en:Ministry of War (pre-modern Japan)|Ministry of War]]. According to the [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]], there were 24 horse pastures in 18 provinces, 12 cattle pastures, and 3 combined horse and cattle pastures, totaling 39 official pastures. Each year, five-year-old horses from these pastures were sent to the {{ill|Left and Right Stables Bureau|ja|左右馬寮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Left and Right Stables Bureau|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, while those from the pastures in the western provinces ([[:en:Saikaidō|Saikaidō]]) were sent to the [[:en:Dazaifu (government)|Dazaifu (Japan)]].  

The '''{{ill|Kintō Maki|ja|近都牧|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kintō Maki|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 諸国牧|1=Shokoku Maki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11632062}}''' were pastures set up to allow these tribute horses and cattle to graze freely. These were primarily located in [[:en:Kyushu|Kyushu]] and around the {{ill|Kinai|ja|近畿|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinai|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近畿地方|1=Kansai region|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q164256}} region.

Under the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]], each pasture was managed by:
- One '''{{ill|Makichō|ja|牧長|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makichō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}''' (chief overseer of the pasture),
- One '''{{ill|Makichō (scribe)|ja|牧帳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makichō (scribe)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}''' (scribe responsible for administrative records),
- Two '''{{ill|Makiko|ja|牧子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makiko|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}''' (caretakers) for every 100 horses or cattle.

The [[:en:Kokushi (official)|Provincial governor]] supervised these officials.  
At the central government level, the {{ill|Hyōbashi|ja|兵馬司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyōbashi|qq=|1=Hyōbashi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11393722}} oversaw all pastures, both state-owned and private. Horses deemed suitable for riding were sent to military garrisons, where wealthier [[:en:Soldier|soldiers]] cared for them, while lower-quality horses were used as relay station mounts. This system ensured state control over the supply and management of cavalry horses.  

If pasture officials (Makichō, Makichō scribes, and Makiko caretakers) allowed the horses to become emaciated, lost animals, or failed to meet the mandated increase of 60 foals per 100 breeding mares, they were punished. Conversely, those who exceeded the quota were rewarded with rice.
